text: On an island, knights always tell the truth and knaves always lie. You meet three people: A, B, and C. A says: "All three of us are knaves." B says: "Exactly one of us is a knight." What are A, B, and C?
explanation: A cannot be a knight — if truthful, 'all knaves' would mean A is a knave (contradiction). So A is a knave. Since A's statement is false, at least one is a knight. If B is knight: 'exactly one knight' is true → only B → C is knave. Check: A(knave) says 'all knaves' — false ✓. B(knight) says 'one knight' — true ✓.

text: You reach a fork in the road. Two guards — one always tells the truth, one always lies. One path leads to freedom, the other to doom. You may ask ONE yes/no question to ONE guard. Which question guarantees the safe path?
choices: Ask: 'If I asked the OTHER guard which path is safe, what would they say?' — then choose the opposite
Ask: 'Would you tell me the left path is safe?'
Ask: 'Does the other guard always lie?'
explanation: Ask either guard: 'If I asked the other guard which path leads to freedom, what would they say?' Truth-teller reports the liar's wrong answer. Liar lies about the truth-teller's correct answer. Both point to the WRONG path. Take the opposite.

text: On the island of knights (truthful) and knaves (liars), you meet A who says: "At least one of us is a knave." What is A?
explanation: If A is knight: 'at least one knave' is true → B is knave. Consistent ✓. If A is knave: statement is false → neither is knave → A is knight. Contradiction! A must be a knight.

text: Knights always tell truth; knaves always lie. A says: "B is a knight." B says: "A and I are different types." What are A and B?
explanation: If A is knight: A tells truth → B is knight. B tells truth → 'different types' → false (both knights). Contradiction! If A is knave: A lies → B is knave. B lies → 'different types' is false → same type → both knaves ✓.

text: Three islanders A, B, C (knights or knaves). A: "B is a knave." B: "A and C are the same type." C: "B is a knave." What is B?
explanation: If B is knight: B tells truth → A and C same type. A says 'B is knave' — false → A is knave. Since same type: C is knave. C says 'B is knave' — false ✓. Consistent. If B is knave: B lies → A≠C. A says 'B is knave' — true → A is knight. C is knave (different from A). C says 'B is knave' — true, but C is knave. Contradiction! B is a knight.
